title: Authors and Citation
text: Soonhong Cho. Author. Doeun Kim. Author. Chad Hazlett. Author, maintainer.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Cho S, Kim D, Hazlett C (2026). gpss: Gaussian Processes Social Science. R package version 1.0.1, https://doeunkim.org/gpss/.
code: @Manual{,   title = {gpss: Gaussian Processes for Social Science},   author = {Soonhong Cho and Doeun Kim and Chad Hazlett},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 1.0.1},   url = {https://doeunkim.org/gpss/}, }

title: Gaussian Processes for Social Science
text: Gaussian Process (GP) combines highly flexible non-linear regression approach rigorous handling uncertainty. key feature approach , can used produce conditional expectation function representing mode posterior conditional distribution, ``choose particular model fit’’ construct uncertainty estimates conditional putting full faith model. valuable uncertainty estimates reflect lesser knowledge locations near, , beyond edge observed data, results approaches become highly model-dependent. first offer accessible explanation GPs, provide implementation suitable social science inference problems, reduces number user-chosen hyperparameters three zero. illustrate settings GPs can valuable: conventional approaches poor properties due model-dependency/extrapolation data-sparse regions. Specifically, demonstrate usefulness GPs contexts () treated control models needed groups poor covariate overlap; (ii) regression discontinuity, depends model estimates taken just beyond edge supporting data; (iii) interrupted time-series designs, models fitted prior event extrapolated .

title: Gaussian Processes for Social Science
text: can install latest version running:
code: devtools::install_github('doeun-kim/gpss')

title: Gaussian Processes for Social Science
text: version uses Rcpp extensively speed reasons. means need right compilers machine.

title: Gaussian Processes for Social Science
text: Windows, need install RTools haven’t already. still difficulty installing says compilation failed, try installing without support multiple architectures:
code: devtools::install_github('doeun-kim/gpss', args=c('--no-multiarch'))

title: Gaussian Processes for Social Science
text: order compile C++ package, RcppArmadillo require compilers installed machine. may already , can install running: may need install armadillo software compile package. can done using Homebrew package manager. problems install Mac OSX, specifically getting errors either lgfortran lquadmath, try open Terminal try following: Also see section 2.16
code: xcode-select --install brew install armadillo curl -O http://r.research.att.com/libs/gfortran-4.8.2-darwin13.tar.bz2 sudo tar fvxz gfortran-4.8.2-darwin13.tar.bz2 -C /

title: Gaussian Processes for Social Science
code: library(gpss) data(lalonde) # categorical variables must be encoded as factors dat <- transform(lalonde, race_ethnicity = factor(race_ethnicity)) # train and test sets idx <- sample(seq_len(nrow(dat)), 500) dat_train <- dat[idx, ] dat_test <- dat[-idx, ] # sample of data for speed mod <- gpss(re78 ~ nsw + age + educ + race_ethnicity, data = dat_train)  #you can use print() and summary() with a gpss object: #print(mod) #summary(mod)  # predictions in the test set p <- predict(mod, dat_test) length(p) #> [1] 6525 head(p) #>            fit       lwr      upr #> [1,] 22408.906 20284.161 24533.65 #> [2,] 17947.976 12033.285 23862.67 #> [3,] 17501.271 14675.931 20326.61 #> [4,]  8826.611 -2288.528 19941.75 #> [5,] 22408.906 20284.161 24533.65 #> [6,] 15825.048 13109.529 18540.57

title: Gaussian Processes for Social Science
code: library(gpss) data(lalonde) cat_vars <- c("race_ethnicity", "married") all_vars <-  c("age","educ","re74","re75","married", "race_ethnicity")  X <- lalonde[,all_vars]   Y <- lalonde$re78 D <- lalonde$nsw  X_train <- X[D==0,] Y_train <- Y[D==0]  X_test <- X[D == 1,] Y_test <- Y[D == 1]  gp_train.out <- gp_train(X = X_train, Y = Y_train, optimize=TRUE,                          mixed_data = TRUE,                           cat_columns = cat_vars)  gp_predict.out <- gp_predict(gp_train.out, X_test)

what: Optional: Accelerate R (faster matrix operations in R)
title: Gaussian Processes for Social Science
text: Mac users can see significant speed (5-10x) using Apple’s native Accelerate BLAS library (vecLib). Upgrade latest version R RStudio, follow steps outlined : Window Users learn details regarding installation reversion, please see blog post. another Blog Post explains benefits using Accelerate BLAS libraries: MacOS MacOS M1.
code: cd /Library/Frameworks/R.framework/Resources/lib  # for vecLib use ln -sf libRblas.vecLib.dylib libRblas.dylib

title: Internal function for placebo checks — .run_placebo_checks
text: Internal function placebo checks

title: Internal function for placebo checks — .run_placebo_checks
code: .run_placebo_checks(   y,   X_full,   n_pre,   kernel_type,   period,   time_col,   scale,   interval_type,   alpha,   optimize,   mixed_data,   cat_columns,   placebo_periods,   verbose )

what: Gaussian Process Interrupted Time Series — gp_its
title: Gaussian Process Interrupted Time Series — gp_its
text: Gaussian Process Interrupted Time Series

title: Gaussian Process Interrupted Time Series — gp_its
code: gp_its(   y,   dates,   date_treat,   covariates = NULL,   kernel_type = "gaussian",   b = NULL,   s2 = 0.3,   period = NULL,   auto_period = FALSE,   time_col = NULL,   scale = TRUE,   optimize = TRUE,   interval_type = c("prediction", "confidence"),   alpha = 0.05,   mixed_data = FALSE,   cat_columns = NULL,   placebo_check = FALSE,   placebo_periods = NULL,   verbose = FALSE )

title: Gaussian Process Interrupted Time Series — gp_its
text: y numeric vector ts object outcome values dates Date vector observation dates date_treat Date treatment/intervention covariates optional matrix/data.frame covariates kernel_type kernel type GP b bandwidth (NULL automatic) s2 noise variance (default 0.3) period period periodic kernels auto_period logical; auto-detect period pre-treatment data time_col character numeric; column time variable scale logical; scale covariates optimize logical; optimize s2 via MLE interval_type "prediction" "confidence" alpha significance level mixed_data logical; whether covariates contain categorical variables cat_columns character vector categorical column names placebo_check logical; run placebo checks placebo_periods number placebo periods (NULL automatic) verbose logical; print progress messages

title: Gaussian Process Interrupted Time Series — gp_its
text: object class "gp_its", list containing: y outcome vector dates date vector date_treat treatment date y0_hat counterfactual predictions post-treatment periods estimates data frame columns tau_t, tau_cum, tau_avg se data frame standard errors ci data frame confidence interval bounds gp_model fitted GP model gp_train placebo_estimates placebo check results (requested)

title: Gaussian Process Interrupted Time Series — gp_its
code: # \donttest{ # Simulated interrupted time series set.seed(42) n <- 50 dates <- seq(as.Date("2020-01-01"), by = "month", length.out = n) y <- rnorm(n) + c(rep(0, 30), rep(2, 20)) res <- gp_its(y, dates, date_treat = as.Date("2022-07-01")) print(res) #> GP-ITS Model #> ----------------------------------------  #> Observations: 50 (pre: 30, post: 20) #> Treatment date: 2022-07-01 #> Kernel: gaussian #> Interval type: prediction (95%) #>  #> Average treatment effect (final period): #>   tau_avg = 1.918 (SE = 0.921) #>   95% CI: [0.113, 3.723] # }

title: gp_predict — gp_predict
text: predict outcome values testing points feeding results obtained gp_train()

title: gp_predict — gp_predict
code: gp_predict(gp, Xtest, prior_mean = NULL)

title: gp_predict — gp_predict
text: gp list-form object obtained gp_train() Xtest data frame matrix testing data set prior_mean numeric vector prior mean values Y test point. Required model trained prior_mean; added Ys_mean_orig recover predictions original Y scale. Must length nrow(Xtest). (default = NULL)

title: gp_predict — gp_predict
text: Xtest_scaled testing data scaled form Xtest original testing data set Ys_mean_scaled predicted values Y scaled form Ys_mean_orig predicted values Y original scale Ys_cov_scaled covariance predicted Y scaled form Ys_cov_orig covariance predicted Y original scale f_cov_orig covariance target function original scale b bandwidth value obtained gp_train() s2 s2 value obtained gp_train()

title: gp_predict — gp_predict
code: # \donttest{ data(lalonde) cat_vars <- c("race_ethnicity", "married") all_vars <- c("age","educ","re74","re75","married", "race_ethnicity")  X <- lalonde[,all_vars] Y <- lalonde$re78 D <- lalonde$nsw  X_train <- X[D==0,] Y_train <- Y[D==0] X_test <- X[D == 1,] Y_test <- Y[D == 1]  gp_train.out <- gp_train(X = X_train, Y = Y_train, optimize=TRUE, mixed_data = TRUE, cat_columns = cat_vars) gp_predict.out <- gp_predict(gp_train.out, X_test) # }

title: gp_rdd — gp_rdd
text: function implements regression discontinuity (RD) design using Gaussian Process (GP) regression side cutoff. Separate GP models estimated observations threshold, allowing flexible fully nonparametric functional forms. treatment effect computed difference predicted conditional means cutoff right left limits. Standard errors confidence intervals constructed using posterior predictive variance two independently fitted GPs.

title: gp_rdd — gp_rdd
code: gp_rdd(   X,   Y,   cut,   alpha = 0.05,   b = NULL,   trim = FALSE,   trim_k_value = 0.1,   scale = TRUE )

title: gp_rdd — gp_rdd
text: X forcing variable Y Y vector (outcome variable) cut cut point alpha confidence level (default = 0.05) b bandwidth (default = NULL) trim logical value indicating whether want automatic trim specific value trim_k_value (default=FALSE) trim_k_value numerical value indicating kernel value want trim (default = 0.1) scale logical value indicating whether want scale covariates (default = TRUE)

title: gp_rdd — gp_rdd
text: tau estimated treatment effect se standard error tau

title: gp_rdd — gp_rdd
code: n <- 100 tau <- 3 cut <- 0 x <- rnorm(n, 0, 1) y <- rnorm(n, 0, 1) + tau*ifelse(x>cut, 1, 0) gp_rdd.out <- gp_rdd(x, y, cut) gp_rdd_plot(gp_rdd.out)

title: gp_rdd_plot — gp_rdd_plot
text: draw RD plot using results obtained gp_rdd()

title: gp_rdd_plot — gp_rdd_plot
code: gp_rdd_plot(gp_rdd_res, l_col = "blue", r_col = "red")

title: gp_rdd_plot — gp_rdd_plot
text: gp_rdd_res list-form results obtained gp_rdd() l_col character value indicating color left side cutoff point (default = "blue") r_col character value indicating color right side cutoff point (default = "red")

title: gp_rdd_plot — gp_rdd_plot
text: ggplot object showing RD plot.

title: gp_rdd_plot — gp_rdd_plot
code: library(ggplot2) n <- 100 tau <- 3 cut <- 0 x <- rnorm(n, 0, 1) y <- rnorm(n, 0, 1) + tau*ifelse(x>cut, 1, 0) gp_rdd.out <- gp_rdd(x, y, cut) gp_rdd_plot(gp_rdd.out) +  geom_vline(xintercept = cut, lty="dashed")

title: gp_train — gp_train
text: train GP model training data set

title: gp_train — gp_train
code: gp_train(   X,   Y,   b = NULL,   s2 = 0.3,   optimize = FALSE,   scale = TRUE,   kernel_type = "gaussian",   period = NULL,   time_col = NULL,   mixed_data = FALSE,   cat_columns = NULL,   Xtest = NULL,   prior_mean = NULL )

title: gp_train — gp_train
text: X set covariate data frame matrix Y Y vector (outcome variable) b bandwidth (default = NULL) s2 noise fraction Y explained X (default = 0.3) optimize logical value indicate whether automatic optimized value S2 used. FALSE, users must define s2. (default = FALSE) scale logical value indicate whether covariates scaled. (default = TRUE) kernel_type character value indicating kernel type (default = "gaussian") period numeric value period parameter, required periodic kernels (default = NULL) time_col character numeric value indicating column time variable. specified, column moved first position correct period scaling periodic kernels. (default = NULL) mixed_data logical value indicate whether covariates contain categorical/binary variable (default = FALSE) cat_columns character numerical vector indicating categorical variables. Must character (numeric) time_col specified. (default = NULL) Xtest data frame matrix testing covariates. necessary non-overlapping categorical value exists training testing data sets. (default = NULL) prior_mean numeric vector prior mean values Y training observation. provided, GP fitted residuals (Y - prior_mean), prior mean added back recover predictions original Y scale. Must length Y. (default = NULL)

title: gp_train — gp_train

title: gp_train — gp_train
code: # \donttest{ data(lalonde) cat_vars <- c("race_ethnicity", "married") all_vars <- c("age","educ","re74","re75","married", "race_ethnicity")  X <- lalonde[,all_vars] Y <- lalonde$re78 D <- lalonde$nsw  X_train <- X[D==0,] Y_train <- Y[D==0] X_test <- X[D == 1,] Y_test <- Y[D == 1]  gp_train.out <- gp_train(X = X_train, Y = Y_train, optimize=TRUE, mixed_data = TRUE, cat_columns = cat_vars) gp_predict.out <- gp_predict(gp_train.out, X_test) # }

what: gpss: Gaussian Processes for Social Science — gpss
title: gpss: Gaussian Processes for Social Science — gpss
text: Provides Gaussian Process (GP) regression tools social science inference problems. GPs combine flexible nonparametric regression principled uncertainty quantification: rather committing single model fit, posterior reflects lesser knowledge edge beyond observed data, approaches become highly model-dependent. package reduces user-chosen hyperparameters three zero supplies convenience functions regression discontinuity ('gp_rdd'), interrupted time-series ('gp_its'), general GP fitting ('gpss', 'gp_train', 'gp_predict'). Methods described Cho, Kim, Hazlett (2026) doi:10.1017/pan.2026.10032 .

title: gpss: Gaussian Processes for Social Science — gpss
text: Maintainer: Chad Hazlett chazlett@ucla.edu Authors: Soonhong Cho tnsehdtm@gmail.com Doeun Kim doeun2@ucla.edu (ORCID)

title: gpss: Gaussian Processes for Social Science — gpss
code: # \donttest{ # -- Basic fitting and prediction ----------------------------- library(gpss) data(lalonde) dat <- transform(lalonde, race_ethnicity = factor(race_ethnicity))  idx <- sample(seq_len(nrow(dat)), 500) mod <- gpss(re78 ~ nsw + age + educ + race_ethnicity, data = dat[idx, ]) p   <- predict(mod, dat[-idx, ]) head(p) #>             fit        lwr      upr #> [1,] 19603.7203  17317.980 21889.46 #> [2,] 17517.0400  12644.866 22389.21 #> [3,] 21435.1890  19262.500 23607.88 #> [4,] 17832.7472  15209.686 20455.81 #> [5,] 13077.4249   8776.605 17378.24 #> [6,]   360.2888 -11517.823 12238.40  # -- G-computation for the ATT (LaLonde data) ---------------- # The LaLonde dataset pairs 185 treated units from the NSW # job-training program with 2490 PSID control units.  The # experimental benchmark ATT is about $1794, but the naive # difference in means is severely biased (~-$15k) due to # poor covariate overlap. # # Strategy: fit a GP on the control group to learn E[Y(0)|X], # predict counterfactual Y(0) for the treated, and compute # ATT = mean(Y_obs - Y0_hat) with a Neyman-style SE. # See Cho, Kim, and Hazlett (2026, Political Analysis).  # Use gp_train / gp_predict for direct control over covariates cat_vars <- c("black", "hisp", "married", "nodegr", "u74", "u75") all_vars <- c("age", "educ", "re74", "re75",               "black", "hisp", "married", "nodegr", "u74", "u75")  X <- lalonde[, all_vars] Y <- lalonde$re78 D <- lalonde$nsw  # Fit GP on control group (optimize s2 via MLE) gp_mod <- gp_train(X = X[D == 0, ], Y = Y[D == 0],                    optimize = TRUE,                    mixed_data = TRUE, cat_columns = cat_vars)  # Predict E[Y(0) | X] for treated units gp_pred <- gp_predict(gp_mod, Xtest = X[D == 1, ])  Y1     <- Y[D == 1]                    # observed Y(1) Y0_hat <- gp_pred$Ys_mean_orig         # predicted E[Y(0)|X] tau_i  <- Y1 - Y0_hat                  # unit-level effects att    <- mean(tau_i)                   # ATT point estimate  # -- Neyman-style SE for the ATT -- # The estimator is a difference of two means: # ATT = mean(Y_obs - Y0_hat). # So a Neyman-style SE: # Var(mean(Y_obs)) + Var(mean(Y0_hat)). # # Two sources of uncertainty: # # 1. Sampling variance of observed outcomes: var(Y1) / n1 # # 2. Posterior uncertainty in the counterfactuals: because Y(0) #    predictions are correlated across units through the GP #    posterior, we need the full covariance matrix V0 = f_cov_orig #    (the posterior covariance of E[Y(0)|X] at treated X values). #    Its contribution to Var(ATT) is (1/n1^2) * sum(V0), #    i.e. 1'V0 1 / n1^2.  n1     <- sum(D) V0     <- gp_pred$f_cov_orig se_att <- sqrt(var(Y1) / n1 + sum(V0) / n1^2)  cat(sprintf("GP ATT:  $%.0f (SE = $%.0f)\n", att, se_att)) #> GP ATT:  $1907 (SE = $1739) cat(sprintf("95%% CI: [$%.0f, $%.0f]\n",             att - 1.96 * se_att, att + 1.96 * se_att)) #> 95% CI: [$-1502, $5316] # GP estimate is close to the experimental benchmark (~$1794); # the wide CI reflects genuine extrapolation uncertainty. # }

what: Data from National Supported Work program and Panel Study in Income Dynamics — lalonde
title: Data from National Supported Work program and Panel Study in Income Dynamics — lalonde
text: Dehejia Wahba (1999) sample data Lalonde (1986). data set includes 185 treated units National Supported Work (NSW) program, paired 2490 control units drawn Panel Study Income Dynamics (PSID-1). treatment variable interest nsw, indicates individual job training program. main outcome interest real earnings 1978 (re78).  remaining variables characteristics individuals, used controls.

title: predict method for gpss objects — predict.gpss
code: # \donttest{ library(gpss) data(lalonde) # categorical variables must be encoded as factors dat <- transform(lalonde, race_ethnicity = factor(race_ethnicity)) # train and test sets idx <- sample(seq_len(nrow(dat)), 500) dat_train <- dat[idx, ] dat_test <- dat[-idx, ] # Fit model mod <- gpss(re78 ~ nsw + age + educ + race_ethnicity, data = dat_train) p <- predict(mod, newdata = dat_test) p_confidence99 <- predict(mod, newdata = dat_test, interval = "confidence", level = 0.99) # }

title: gpss 1.0.0
text: Initial CRAN release. Core GP functions: gp_train(), gp_predict(), formula interface gpss() predict() method. Regression discontinuity design via gp_rdd() gp_rdd_plot(). Interrupted time-series design via gp_its() optional placebo checks. Multiple kernel types: Gaussian, Gaussian-linear, Gaussian-quadratic, Gaussian-periodic-linear, Gaussian-periodic-quadratic. Automatic bandwidth selection via kernel variance maximization. Optional noise variance optimization via marginal likelihood. Support mixed continuous/categorical covariates. Prior mean support incorporating external predictions. Companion paper: Cho, Kim, Hazlett (2026), “Inference data’s edge: Gaussian processes modeling inference model-dependency, poor overlap, extrapolation,” Political Analysis, doi:10.1017/pan.2026.10032.
